# Bone health & osteoporosis

> Part IV ends where the skeleton does its slow accounting. Bone is a living bank: you pay into it through
> childhood and youth, the balance peaks in your late twenties, and you draw it down for the rest of your life —
> faster in women around menopause. Osteoporosis is that account run low, and it stays silent until a fragility
> fracture announces it. The task here is to **recognise the dietary levers — calcium, vitamin D, protein and
> enough energy — build the bank early, defend the balance lifelong, and refer** — not to prescribe the
> osteoporosis drugs, which are specialist.

## Bone health & osteoporosis at a glance

This chapter closes Part IV as the other half of the deficiency-and-frailty pair it opened with the
*Undernutrition & Malnutrition* chapter — the frail, undernourished, sarcopenic patient and the fracturing
skeleton are two faces of the same late-life vulnerability. Osteoporosis is common, **silent until it breaks**,
and costly: Singapore historically had among the highest hip-fracture rates in Asia, and while the
age-standardised *rate* is now falling, the absolute *number* of fractures keeps rising as the population ages —
a distinction worth holding onto [@koh2001hipfracSG; @yong2019hipfracSG; @chandran2019burdenSG].

Three habits of mind run through it:

- **Build the bank in youth, then defend the balance.** Peak bone mass is essentially set by the late twenties, and only about **20–40% of it is modifiable** by lifestyle — real, but bounded, leverage [@nofPeakBoneMass2016].
- **Bioavailability beats content, and dairy is optional.** How much calcium is *absorbed* — not the number on the label — is the lever, and a multi-ethnic, partly lactose-intolerant population can meet its needs without milk [@weaverVegCalcium1999; @healthhubCalciumSG].
- **Correct real deficiency; don't supplement everyone.** Calcium and vitamin D help people who are genuinely short, but blanket pills for already-replete community adults do not prevent fractures [@zhao2017cavitdNoBenefit].

## The science underneath: bone is a living bank

Why does diet matter for something as solid as bone? Because bone is not inert scaffolding — it is **living
tissue in constant turnover**. Cells called **osteoblasts** lay down new bone while **osteoclasts** resorb old
bone, and the balance between them sets whether the account grows or shrinks. Through childhood and puberty
building outpaces resorption, so the balance climbs to a genetically-set **peak bone mass** by the late twenties
(about 90% of it laid down by the end of adolescence); thereafter resorption gradually wins and the balance
falls for the rest of life [@hereford2024peakBoneMass; @bailey1999saskatchewanAccrual]. Everyone leaks bone —
in both sexes, beginning in young adulthood — which is why the levers matter across the whole life course, not
only after menopause [@riggs2008boneLossSites]. The **building** phase itself — the adolescent bone-building
window, and the low energy availability that can blunt it — is owned by the *Childhood & Adolescence* chapter,
which this chapter picks up from.

<div data-island="bone-bank" data-src="/diagrams/bone-bank.yml"></div>

In women, the withdrawal sharpens around the **menopause**: as oestrogen falls it **raises a resorption signal
called RANKL**, tipping the balance toward the osteoclasts, and women lose roughly 7% of spine and 6% of
femoral-neck bone mineral across the transition [@greendale2012swanBone; @eghbali2003rankl]. The hormonal
context — and menopausal hormone therapy — belongs to the *Menopause & Midlife Health* chapter; here we follow
the diet.

Three nutrients act on this bank, and it helps to be precise about *how* each works — the underlying nutrient
biology is owned by the *Micronutrients, Hydration & Electrolytes* and *Macronutrients* chapters:

- **Calcium is the substrate** — the mineral the bone is built from, drawn from the *Micronutrients, Hydration & Electrolytes* chapter.
- **Vitamin D is the enabler** — it does not build bone directly; its active form drives the **intestinal absorption of calcium**, so a shortfall means calcium passes through uneaten, the parathyroid glands pull calcium back out of bone, and the skeleton pays [@laird2010vitDbone].
- **Protein is the matrix and the scaffold-builder** — about half of bone *volume* is a protein (type-I collagen) framework, and dietary protein also drives the growth factor IGF-1 and preserves the muscle that prevents falls; it is a bone nutrient, not a bone threat (more on the myth below).

(A related pathway runs the other way in disease: when failing kidneys can no longer balance calcium, phosphate
and vitamin D, the resulting **CKD–mineral and bone disease** is the *Chronic Kidney Disease* chapter's —
recognised and referred, not re-taught here.)

The organizing idea, captured in the island above, is that a **higher peak delays the day the account runs
low**. A modelled 10% higher peak bone density delays the onset of osteoporosis by about **13 years** — far
more than later menopause or slower loss buy — but note the units: that is **years of delay, not a percentage
cut in fractures**, and the two must never be conflated [@hernandez2003peakBmdModel].

## Calcium: substrate, and why content isn't the whole story

Singapore's calcium requirements run across the life course: about **1000 mg/day for adolescents (10–18),
800 mg for adults 19–50, and 1000 mg for adults 51 and over** and in pregnancy and lactation [@hpbRDACalcium].
Local intake sits at a mean of **~794 mg/day** — actually among the highest in Asia, where most countries fall
below 400–500 mg, but still under the requirement — and the gap widens with age, with about **half of older
Singaporeans falling short** [@balkGlobalCalcium2017; @hpbNNS2010; @ye2023daha].

The lever, though, is **bioavailability, not content**. How much calcium a food actually delivers depends on
how well it is absorbed, and that varies enormously: the calcium in **spinach is bound to oxalate and only
about 5% absorbed**, versus roughly a quarter to a third from milk, while **calcium-set tofu absorbs comparably
to milk** and low-oxalate greens like kai-lan and bok choy are absorbed *better* still [@odsCalcium;
@weaverTofuMilk2002; @weaverVegCalcium1999]. So the spinach that looks calcium-rich on a label is nearly
useless for bone, and the tofu next to it is excellent — the *Micronutrients, Hydration & Electrolytes* chapter
owns this oxalate contrast.

![How much calcium a food delivers depends on how much is absorbed, not the amount it contains. Calcium in high-oxalate spinach is only about 5% absorbed; milk and calcium-set tofu sit around a quarter to a third; low-oxalate greens like kai-lan and bok choy are absorbed better still. Content is not availability — the spinach that looks calcium-rich is nearly useless for bone.](/figures/mechanisms/calcium-bioavailability.svg){#fig-calcium-bio width="82%" fig-align="center"}

::: {.callout-tip}
## 🍜 Eating out (and eating in) — the dairy-optional calcium plate
Dairy is optional in Singapore. Familiar foods close the gap: **calcium-set tofu and tau kwa** (yong tau foo
picks), **ikan bilis and canned sardines eaten with the soft bones**, **low-oxalate greens** (kai-lan, bok
choy, chye sim), and **fortified soy milk** — HPB lists per-serving calcium (sardines with bones ~270 mg, ikan
bilis ~270 mg, tau kwa ~150 mg, kai-lan ~195 mg, fortified soy milk ~500 mg, versus ~380 mg for a glass of
low-fat milk) [@healthhubCalciumSG]. Small swaps stack up: tofu in the yong tau foo, milk in the kopi or teh,
ikan bilis in the stock. Absorption is best in **doses under ~500 mg at a sitting**, so spread it across the day
[@odsCalcium].
:::

## Vitamin D: the enabler, and Singapore's paradox

Vitamin D earns a section of its own because of a genuinely counterintuitive local fact. Its active form,
made in the skin from sunlight and finished in the liver and kidney, is what lets the gut absorb calcium at all
[@laird2010vitDbone] — yet in **sunny, equatorial Singapore, roughly 1 in 3 mid-life women are vitamin D
deficient** [@cheong2021vitDsg]. The cause is not the climate but **behaviour and biology**: indoor lifestyles,
cosmetic sun avoidance, covering and veiling, and darker skin pigmentation all cut skin synthesis, and the
skin's ability to make vitamin D more than halves with age [@nimitphong_2013_vitd_sea; @maclaughlin1985skinVitD].
The burden is also **unequal** — Indian and Malay women carry several-fold higher odds of deficiency than
Chinese women — which makes it a concrete equity point, not just a biochemistry one [@cheong2021vitDsg].

![Vitamin D from skin and diet is activated through the liver and kidney to its active form, which drives the gut to absorb calcium, keeping bone supplied. When vitamin D is low, calcium absorption falls, parathyroid hormone rises, and calcium is pulled out of bone — so the deficiency starves the skeleton. Vitamin D does not build bone directly; it enables calcium to be absorbed.](/figures/mechanisms/vitamin-d-calcium-axis.svg){#fig-vitd-axis width="92%" fig-align="center"}

The response, though, is *not* to test and dose everyone. Few foods contain vitamin D naturally (oily fish is
the best), and guideline bodies now advise **against routine testing and empiric supplementation in otherwise
healthy adults**; HPB's own vitamin D reference intake is a modest 2.5 µg (100 IU) precisely because it assumes
some sun exposure [@odsVitaminD; @demay2024endoSocVitD; @hpb_healthhub_rda]. The teaching point is that
**insufficiency by the lab cutoff is common, but the answer is to find and correct genuine shortfall in
higher-risk people — the covered, the indoors, the elderly, the pregnant — not to blanket-dose the population**.

## Protein is a bone nutrient, not a bone threat

A persistent myth holds that protein "leaches calcium from bone" — the so-called acid-ash hypothesis. It is
**backwards**. Higher protein intake, *with adequate calcium*, tracks about **11% lower hip-fracture risk**, and
the extra calcium that appears in the urine after a protein meal comes from **better intestinal absorption**,
not from dissolved bone [@groenendijk2019protein]. This makes mechanistic sense: protein is the collagen matrix
bone is built on, it drives the bone-building growth factor IGF-1, and it maintains the muscle that keeps frail
people from falling — the muscle–bone overlap the *Undernutrition & Malnutrition* and *Healthy Ageing* chapters
meet from the sarcopenia side. So the advice for an older patient worried that "meat is bad for my bones" is the opposite of
what they fear: eat enough protein, alongside enough calcium.

## Body weight, and the whole pattern

Two more levers round out the diet. **Adequate body weight is itself protective**: a low BMI (20 versus 25)
nearly *doubles* hip-fracture risk, largely because it tracks lower bone density — so being underweight or
underfuelled is a bone red flag, and one that the frailty of late life makes common [@delaet2005bmiFracture].
And the **whole dietary pattern beats any single nutrient**: plenty of fruit and vegetables (supplying
potassium, magnesium and vitamin K), a reliable calcium source, enough protein and enough energy — the overall
pattern the *What a Healthy Diet Looks Like* chapter owns [@iofNutritionProf].

## The contested box: solid risks versus weak claims

Some foods get blamed for bones on thin evidence, and it is worth separating the solid from the soft. The
**solid** dietary risks are low calcium, vitamin D, protein or body weight, plus heavy alcohol; smoking is a
major (non-dietary) risk [@iofRiskFactors; @ke2023alcoholFracture; @law1997smokingHipFracture]. The **contested**
trio is weaker than the headlines suggest: heavy **salt** genuinely raises urinary calcium, but its fracture
link is weak and the real reason to cut salt is blood pressure; **caffeine's** tiny effect is offset by the
splash of milk in the coffee; and the **cola–bone** signal is observational and confounded by milk displacement,
not proven acid damage [@teucher2008sodiumBone; @heaney2002caffeineBone; @tucker2006colaBMD]. Recognise which
box a claim belongs in before repeating it.

## Supplements: correct deficiency, don't dose everyone

This is the chapter's most important caveat, because it is where good intentions go wrong.
Calcium and vitamin D supplements **do** cut fractures in people who are genuinely deficient or institutionalised
— by roughly 15% for all fractures and 30% for hip fractures in that group [@weaver2016cavitd]. But in
**already-replete, community-dwelling older adults**, large trials and pooled analyses find **no fracture
benefit at all**, and a small *increase* in kidney stones [@zhao2017cavitdNoBenefit; @leboff2022vitalFractures;
@uspstfCalcium2018]. So the message is not "everyone over 50 should take calcium and vitamin D." It is **food
first, correct genuine deficiency, and reserve supplements for real shortfall or high risk** — the recognise,
don't-blanket-treat posture.

::: {.callout-note}
## The strongest modifiable lever isn't a food
Diet builds and defends the bank, but the single strongest *modifiable* signal to bone is **mechanical
loading** — weight-bearing and resistance exercise. In a landmark trial, supervised high-intensity resistance
and impact training actually *raised* bone density in postmenopausal women with low bone mass, safely
[@watson2018liftmor]. Nutrition and loading are partners — calcium is the substrate, loading is the signal to
use it — so name the exercise lever and let the *physical-activity* side own the prescription; HPB's own public
message pairs a high-calcium diet with weight-bearing exercise [@hpbOsteoporosisAwareness2009].
:::

## The Singapore picture

**The paradox and the gap.** The signature local facts are the **vitamin D paradox** (common deficiency in a
sunny country, driven by behaviour and pigmentation and unequal across ethnic groups) and the **calcium gap**
(~794 mg/day on average, with about half of older adults short) — both covered above [@cheong2021vitDsg;
@ye2023daha]. Together they make the "sunny country, dairy-rich West" assumptions fail here, which is why the
dairy-optional, food-first Asian calcium list matters.

**The fracture burden — rate versus count.** Singapore's hip-fracture *rates* have been **falling** since 2000
(and continued to 2022), yet absolute *cases* keep **rising** with the ageing population, and osteoporotic
fractures are projected to climb further by 2035 [@yong2019hipfracSG; @aceOsteoporosis2025; @chandran2019burdenSG].
Fractures fall about **2:1 in women versus men** and **Chinese** carry the highest ethnic risk
[@yong2019hipfracSG], and a hip fracture is genuinely **lethal** — mortality runs about double the background rate in women and higher in men
[@yong2020mortalitySG]. Teach the rate-versus-count distinction explicitly: things are improving per person and
worsening in total.

**The guidance stack you signpost.** HPB's simple public message is two levers — a **high-calcium diet plus
weight-bearing exercise** [@hpbOsteoporosisAwareness2009]. Case-finding uses **OSTA** (an Asian age-and-weight
screen that flags who needs a scan) and **FRAX** (a 10-year fracture-probability tool with Singapore-calibrated
models); a **DXA** bone scan showing a T-score of −2.5 or below (2.5 standard deviations under the young-adult
peak) — or any fragility fracture (a break from a fall from standing height or less) — defines
osteoporosis [@koh2001osta; @fraxSingapore; @aceOsteoporosis2018]. The drug treatment and the menopausal hormone
decisions are **specialist**, guided by the national **ACE** guidance and the **2025 Singapore consensus** —
signposted, not run from here [@aceOsteoporosis2025; @gani2025osteoconsensusSG].

::: {.callout-important}
## You recognise the levers and refer — you don't prescribe the osteoporosis drugs
There is no drug regimen to memorise here. The generalist **recognises the dietary levers** (calcium, vitamin
D, protein, adequate energy), delivers the **food-first** message and the loading message, **corrects genuine
deficiency** rather than blanket-supplementing, spots the **red flags** (a fragility fracture, low body weight,
a covered indoor patient, the frail faller), and **refers** — using OSTA/FRAX/DXA to decide who needs
assessment. The bone-density scans, the fracture-risk thresholds, the bisphosphonates and the hormone therapy
are specialist territory [@aceOsteoporosis2018; @gani2025osteoconsensusSG].
:::

## So — how do you keep a skeleton strong?

You build the bank while it is still being built — calcium, vitamin D, protein and activity through childhood
and youth — and then you defend the balance for the rest of life: a reliable, well-absorbed calcium source
(dairy optional), enough vitamin D (corrected where genuinely short, not blanket-dosed), enough protein and
enough energy, weight-bearing exercise, and less alcohol and tobacco. You recognise the frail, underweight or
covered patient at risk, you correct real deficiency without medicalising the whole population, and you refer
for the scan and the specialist decisions. With that, Part IV is complete — from the diseases of *too much*
through the cardiometabolic-renal cascade to the deficiency-and-frailty pair of *too little* — and the through
line holds: across the whole life course, what and how a person eats shapes whether they stay well.

## Check your understanding

<div data-island="quiz" data-src="/quizzes/bone-health-osteoporosis.quiz.yml"></div>

## Practise: Madam Lim and the dairy-free bones

Madam Lim is 58, three years past menopause, and Chinese. She is lactose-intolerant so avoids milk, works and
stays mostly indoors, covers up and walks little; her BMI is 19. Her diet is mostly rice and vegetables with a
little tofu and not much fish, and she has had no fracture — yet. She's read that she should "take calcium and
vitamin D tablets like everyone my age," that "spinach is full of calcium," and wonders whether she needs a
drug. Work through the visit: find her biggest dietary lever, handle the bioavailability and blanket-supplement
traps, weigh her low body weight, and decide the right next step — recognising where reaching for a drug, rather
than screening and referring, would be the wrong reflex. Your choices branch the case.

<div data-island="case" data-src="/cases/madam-lim-bone.case.yml"></div>
